prompt:
Exploring Horizontal Gene Transfer (HGT) in Plants and animals (ie Non-Microbial Systems)
You could examine instances of horizontal gene transfer in eukaryotes—particularly plants and animals—and evaluate the evolutionary significance of these transfers. Its very rare and therefore must have a really interesting reason behind this adaptation!
Especially as this horizontal gene transfer has been well -studied in microbial systems, but not in plants and animals (this is a relatively new discovery).  Understanding  how commonly genes move between eukaryotic species and whether these transfers confer benefits would be really interesting to find out
article:
### What counts as HGT in plants/animals (and why it *looks* rarer than in microbes)
**Horizontal gene transfer (HGT)** is the movement of genetic material between organisms **not via parent–offspring inheritance** (i.e., not through mating/reproduction) [[21]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38263430/). In microbes, HGT is frequent because DNA uptake, conjugation, transduction, and recombination are common and often immediately beneficial.

In **multicellular eukaryotes (plants/animals)**, *heritable* HGT is intrinsically harder because a transferred sequence must typically:
1) **enter recipient cells**,  
2) **reach the nucleus (or stably replace an organelle genome)**,  
3) **integrate** in a way compatible with chromosome/replication biology,  
4) **end up in a lineage that contributes to offspring** (germline or germline-equivalent), and  
5) **avoid purifying selection** and sometimes acquire workable expression/splicing/regulation [[11]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4632278/), [[35]](https://dash.harvard.edu/bitstreams/7312037c-64c1-6bd4-e053-0100007fdf3b/download), [[36]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27139503/).

That chain of constraints is why genuinely inherited HGT is **rare overall** in animals (especially those with early germline sequestration) and why many “spectacular” claims later turned out to be **contamination, misassembly, or taxonomic/annotation artifacts** [[41]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s13059-022-02619-9), [[49]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10898089/). Yet, the cases that *do* persist are often highly informative because they illuminate **unusual ecological interfaces and molecular vectors** (parasites, symbionts, viruses, graft unions) and because selection can amplify a rare event into a major adaptation.

---

## 1) Strong, well-validated case studies (plants)

### A. Natural “genetic engineering” by *Agrobacterium* → plant nuclear genomes (benchmark interkingdom HGT)
This is the **best-understood, repeatedly validated** mechanism of natural interkingdom gene transfer into plants.

**Key points**
- *Agrobacterium* transfers a defined DNA segment (**T-DNA**) into plant cells and integrates it into the **plant nuclear genome** through non-homologous recombination [[28]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4127661/).  
- Multiple plant lineages carry inherited T-DNA fragments (“cellular T-DNA”, **cT-DNA**)—i.e., natural transformants [[28]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4127661/), [[29]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5606197/).

**Canonical examples**
- **Cultivated sweet potato (*Ipomoea batatas*)**: two different T-DNA regions (**IbT‑DNA1/2**) are present in cultivated genomes, and **T-DNA genes are expressed** in multiple tissues; IbT‑DNA1 appears in **all 291** surveyed cultivated accessions and not in close wild relatives, consistent with (at least) a strong association with domestication history [[27]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4426443/).  
- **Nicotiana** and **Linaria** species: multiple independent cT-DNA insertions have been described, including expressed rol/opine-related genes, implying long-term persistence [[28]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4127661/), [[29]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5606197/).

**Why it matters evolutionarily**
- It demonstrates that **stable, inherited interkingdom DNA transfer** can happen naturally—and provides a **mechanistic template** for understanding when/why cross-kingdom HGT is possible in plants [[28]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4127661/), [[27]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4426443/).  
- It also reframes “naturalness” arguments in biotech regulation and risk perception because a major food crop is, in this sense, “naturally transgenic” [[27]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4426443/).

---

### B. Parasitic plant ↔ host HGT (especially mitochondria, but also nuclear)
Parasitic plants form **haustoria** that create intimate physiological connections; transcripts and macromolecules can move bidirectionally, and these interfaces are repeatedly implicated as HGT “hotspots” [[1]](https://www.mdpi.com/2075-1729/11/8/857), [[20]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8401529/).

#### B1. Nuclear HGT into parasitic plants (genes; sometimes large blocks)
**Genome-wide evidence in parasites**
- **Rafflesia (parasite) transcriptome vs host (*Tetrastigma*)**: dozens of **actively transcribed** genes in the parasite show phylogenomic placement consistent with host origin, exceeding background discordance rates and argued not to be simple contamination [[4]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3460754/).  
- **Cuscuta campestris genome**: the genome paper explicitly reports evidence for **horizontal gene transfer by genomic DNA integration from hosts** [[89]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-018-04344-z). (The excerpted material establishes the claim and genome context; detailed candidate lists and locus-by-locus validation live in the full paper/supplements.)  
- **Orobanchaceae (obligate parasites) genome survey**: among five parasitic species, **106 host-derived genes** were detected **only in the two obligate parasites**; importantly, most retained **introns at the same positions as host homologs**, supporting **DNA-mediated transfer** rather than an RNA intermediate; some inferred transfers may involve **very large segments (>100 kb)** [[90]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30407540/), [[91]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6294234/). The estimated nuclear HGT proportion was on the order of **0.1–0.2% of genes** in those obligate parasites [[90]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30407540/), [[20]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8401529/).

**Why this is a big deal**
- These cases imply that parasitism can enable **germline-relevant DNA entry** in plants (plants don’t always have a permanently sequestered germline in the same way animals do, and reproductive tissues can derive from somatic lineages—so somatic acquisitions may be more “rescuable” by reproduction in principle).  
- Large-block transfer and intron retention strongly argue against many trivial artifact explanations (e.g., “it’s just an mRNA contaminant”) [[90]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30407540/), [[91]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6294234/).

#### B2. Mitochondrial HGT in parasitic plant systems (frequent; sometimes extreme)
Plant mitochondria are unusually prone to HGT, likely because of their biology (recombination, fusion/networking) [[3]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1114076109). Many classic plant-to-plant HGT examples involve **mitochondrial genes** [[1]](https://www.mdpi.com/2075-1729/11/8/857).

**Extreme canonical case (not necessarily parasitic): *Amborella trichopoda***
- Early multi-gene phylogenetics suggested *Amborella* acquired foreign versions of **20/31** mitochondrial protein genes (26 foreign genes total) from diverse land plants, including multiple moss donors [[70]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15598737/).  
- Complete mitogenome sequencing later revealed a ~**3.9 Mb** mitochondrial genome containing **multiple genome equivalents of foreign mtDNA**, including acquisition of **entire mitochondrial genomes** from green algae and a moss, supporting massive organelle-level capture and recombination [[83]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24357311/).  
- A “fusion-compatibility” model was proposed: *Amborella* may capture whole mitochondria, followed by fusion and recombination, aided by low DNA loss rate and ecological exposure [[83]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24357311/).

**Rafflesiaceae mitochondria**
- A high-profile study inferred **24–41%** of sampled mitochondrial gene sequences show HGT signals in Rafflesiaceae, with many intact reading frames and **active transcription**, and contigs containing both native and foreign sequences supporting integration [[92]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3573108/).  
- Later discussion notes that whether some extreme parasitic mitochondrial HGT inferences are truly foreign and/or functional has been **questioned**, highlighting ongoing debate around the most spectacular fractions [[84]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8588681/).

**A useful counterexample: Cuscuta mitochondria**
- Complete mitogenomes of **Cuscuta australis/campestris** show **little to no mitochondrial HGT**, surprising given nuclear HGT signals in Cuscuta; a smaller mitogenome was suggested as one possible limiting factor [[84]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8588681/).  
- This matters because it shows parasitism does not guarantee mitochondrial HGT; lineage-specific constraints still matter.

---

### C. Grafting and “organelle capture”: whole chloroplast genomes can move between species
A striking and experimentally demonstrated plant-specific route is **horizontal movement of organelles (and their genomes)** across **graft junctions**, potentially explaining puzzling phylogenetic patterns where chloroplast genomes appear “captured” without nuclear introgression.

**Key experimental results (Nicotiana grafts)**
- By grafting sexually incompatible **Nicotiana** species, researchers showed **complete chloroplast genomes** can cross a graft junction and replace resident plastids **without recombination**—a plausible mechanism for chloroplast capture [[3]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1114076109), [[25]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3289295/).  
- A later mechanistic study visualized how this could work: at graft unions, wounding triggers callus proliferation; plastids can dedifferentiate into **small, motile amoeboid organelles**, and newly formed intercellular pores allow plastid movement into neighboring cells. The work argues all three genomes (nuclear, plastid, mitochondrial) can participate in horizontal genome transfer at graft sites [[26]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7775762/).

**Evolutionary significance**
- Provides a concrete, non-sexual mechanism to generate **cytonuclear discordance** (chloroplast phylogeny not matching nuclear phylogeny) that does *not* require hybridization, addressing a long-standing puzzle in plant phylogeography and systematics [[25]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3289295/).  
- Offers a plausible route for **asexual acquisition of organelle genotypes** across otherwise strong reproductive barriers [[25]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3289295/), [[26]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7775762/).

---

### D. Nuclear HGT among grasses (Poaceae): rare overall, but can drive major functional shifts
Recent work highlights that among non-parasitic plants, **reported HGT is concentrated in grasses** (though this might partly reflect research intensity and genome availability) [[39]](https://hal.science/hal-05307487/document).

**Functional “shortcut” example: C4 photosynthesis optimization**
- In **Alloteropsis**, laterally acquired versions of **phosphoenolpyruvate carboxylase (PEPC)** from distantly related C4 lineages show enhanced catalytic properties consistent with having evolved in older C4 systems before transfer—an “evolutionary shortcut” to improved C4 biochemistry [[74]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7751175/).

**How common within a lineage can it be?**
- A population/genus-scale analysis reported **168 laterally acquired genes** across five Alloteropsis reference genomes, with inferred gain rates of ~**6–28 per million years**, rapid subsequent losses, and many laterally acquired genes behaving as **accessory genes** in a pangenome [[40]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37793435/). This frames HGT as a contributor to standing gene-content variation and local adaptation in some plant radiations [[40]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37793435/).

(Additional landmark grass papers are widely cited in later syntheses—e.g., transfers of nuclear ribosomal genes and large DNA fragments among grasses [[86]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1613375114), [[87]](https://www.labex-tulip.fr/eng/the-labex/research/research-highlights/2019-research-highlights/lateral-transfers-of-functional-genes-among-grasses)—but those details are not fully present in the excerpts gathered here, so the most defensible functional mechanism to emphasize from the captured material is the Alloteropsis PEPC/turnover work [[74]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7751175/), [[40]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37793435/).)

---

### E. Transposable element (TE) horizontal transfer in plants: likely far more common than gene HGT
Even if protein-coding HGT is rare, **TE horizontal transfer** can be relatively common and can reshape genome architecture.

- A genome-scale search across **40 plant genomes** found **32 cases** of horizontal transfer of LTR retrotransposons; **65%** of genomes had at least one case, and transferred elements could remain functional and trigger transposition bursts [[33]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4009612/).  
- Mechanistic plausibility: TEs form extrachromosomal intermediates; some resemble viruses (envelope-like domains), potentially making them more vector-friendly [[32]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2633806/).

**Evolutionary significance**
- TE HT can drive **genome expansion**, **regulatory rewiring**, and structural evolution, even when direct phenotypic effects are hard to map [[33]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4009612/), [[32]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2633806/).

---

## 2) Strong, well-validated case studies (animals)

### A. Metabolic/digestive enzyme acquisitions enabling new diets or niches
These are among the clearest examples where HGT confers an immediately interpretable adaptive advantage.

#### A1. Coffee berry borer beetle (*Hypothenemus hampei*): bacterial mannanase
- A beetle gene (**HhMAN1**) shows strong evidence of bacterial origin and encodes a **mannanase** (rare/previously unknown in insects). Recombinant enzyme hydrolyzes **coffee galactomannan**, a major storage polysaccharide in coffee beans [[7]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22371593/), [[59]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3306691/).  
- The gene is widespread across global accessions but absent in a close relative that does not colonize coffee beans, consistent with a niche-specific adaptation [[7]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22371593/).

#### A2. Whitefly (*Bemisia tabaci*): plant detoxification gene (plant → insect HGT)
- A **Cell** study reports whiteflies acquired a **plant-derived phenolic glucoside malonyltransferase** (**BtPMaT1**) enabling detoxification of plant phenolic glucosides [[60]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867421001641).  
- Functional validation is unusually strong: plants engineered to produce siRNAs silencing BtPMaT1 impaired whitefly detoxification and made tomato **fully resistant** [[60]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867421001641).  
This is one of the cleanest “HGT → phenotype → ecological outcome” chains in animals.

---

### B. Pigment biosynthesis from fungi → arthropods: aphids (and spider mites)
Animals usually obtain carotenoids from diet, so endogenous carotenoid synthesis is striking.

#### B1. Aphids: fungal carotenoid genes and visible phenotype
- A **Science** paper reports aphids possess **fungal-derived carotenoid biosynthesis genes**, integrated and duplicated in the aphid genome [[56]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20431015/).  
- A specific genomic region (including a desaturase) correlates with a red/green polymorphism; mutation alters carotenoid profile and body color—direct genotype-to-phenotype mapping [[56]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20431015/).  
Because color affects susceptibility to natural enemies, this provides a plausible ecological selection pathway [[56]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20431015/).

#### B2. Spider mite (*Tetranychus urticae*): independently detected fungal carotenoid genes
- A **Biology Letters** study identified two fungal-like carotenoid genes in spider mites; genomic scaffold context supported that these are truly in the mite genome, and expression differed between morphs and diapause states [[57]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3297373/).  
This supports repeated or broader arthropod use of fungal carotenoid pathways after transfer.

---

### C. Plant-parasitic nematodes: bacterial/fungal CAZymes as a key innovation enabling plant parasitism
Plant cell walls are a formidable barrier; animals typically don’t encode the needed enzymes.

- A landmark **PNAS** paper demonstrated cyst nematodes produce endogenous **cellulases** (glycosyl hydrolase family 5), expressed in esophageal gland cells with secretory signal peptides and eukaryotic mRNA features (polyadenylation), and with close similarity to bacterial cellulases—supporting HGT [[58]](https://edepot.wur.nl/658009).  
- An EST-based screen in root-knot nematodes identified additional HGT candidates, verified as bona fide nematode genes, implicating rhizobial donors and linking HGT to parasitism [[63]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC193618/).  
- A multi-species analysis found cellulase genes correlate perfectly with **measured cellulase activity** across species, consistent with functional assimilation; patterns indicate multiple independent acquisitions and subsequent gene turnover under selection [[62]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3032686/).  
- The **Meloidogyne incognita** genome paper emphasizes an unprecedented diversity of plant cell wall-degrading enzymes, interpreted as deriving from multiple bacterial HGTs [[94]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18660804/).  
- A focused review synthesizes these as repeated independent acquisitions that became essential parasitism tools, followed by duplications suggesting selection for expanded arsenals [[75]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3337135/).

**Evolutionary significance**
HGT here appears to have enabled **major niche transitions** (animal lineages becoming successful plant parasites) by importing “plug-in” enzymatic capabilities [[75]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3337135/), [[62]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3032686/).

---

### D. Virus-mediated gene flow in insects: bracoviruses as gene transfer agents (wasp ↔ lepidopteran)
- Bracoviruses are symbiotic viruses integrated in parasitoid wasps; viral circles are injected into lepidopteran hosts and normally integrate into host somatic cells. Evidence shows bracovirus sequences have been inserted **repeatedly into lepidopteran genomes**, implying occasional germline entry [[8]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4574769/).  
- Some transferred genes (e.g., C-type lectins) likely originate from wasp genes, supporting flux between insect orders mediated by the virus [[8]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4574769/).  
- The paper reports domestication/expression of bracovirus-derived sequences in Lepidoptera and presents functional evidence consistent with protection against baculovirus pathogens—linking HGT to arms-race dynamics [[8]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4574769/).

This is a rare case where **the vector and integration mechanism are unusually explicit** in animals.

---

### E. Endosymbiont-to-host DNA integration: Wolbachia fragments in animal nuclear genomes
Many transfers may be nonfunctional, but they demonstrate frequent DNA leakage/integration opportunities.

- In a beetle (**Callosobruchus chinensis**), ~**30%** of Wolbachia genes (hundreds of kb) appear integrated into the host X chromosome; evidence supports a single large transfer event, but the inserted genes are largely disrupted and transcriptionally inactive (pseudogenized) [[6]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2203625/).  
- Reviews emphasize Wolbachia’s intracellular, maternally transmitted lifestyle (often residing near/within germline cells) makes it an “ideal donor” for heritable transfers [[31]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/).

Evolutionarily, these events often look like **genomic fossils**—important for understanding mechanism and frequency, even when not adaptive.

---

### F. Bdelloid rotifers: exceptional foreign gene content and functional deployment (but also a cautionary tale)
Bdelloids are famous because they combine (i) unusual life history (often asexual, stress tolerant) and (ii) high reported foreign gene content.

**Evidence for substantial functional foreign gene expression**
- Transcriptome-based work in **Adineta ricciae** reports many expressed genes of non-metazoan origin; ~80% of expressed foreign genes encode enzymes, representing ~39% of enzymes in identified pathways, supporting a major metabolic contribution [[61]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23166508/). Physical linkage of foreign genes with metazoan genes in the genome was used to argue against contamination [[61]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23166508/), [[64]](https://journals.plos.org/plosgenetics/article).  
- Across **Rotaria** species, many foreign genes are shared (implying ancient acquisitions) while many are lineage-specific (implying ongoing gain/loss), with higher HGT levels in desiccation-prone species but substantial foreign gene presence even in aquatic species [[11]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4632278/).  
- A 2024 study links HGT to biological conflict: under fungal pathogen challenge, horizontally acquired genes are disproportionately upregulated; notably, clusters resembling bacterial antibiotic biosynthesis pathways are enriched and strongly induced in resistant species [[76]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-49919-1).

**What is *not* supported (artifact)**
- A separate claim of frequent *between-bdelloid* DNA transfers based on field-sampled locus discordance was refuted: reanalysis showed mixed-template contamination (multiple animals’ DNA) explains the patterns [[12]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30017483/), [[101]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960982218307061).  
This split underscores a general point: **“foreign genes in bdelloid genomes”** is plausible and supported by multiple genome/transcriptome analyses [[11]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4632278/), [[61]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23166508/), whereas **“routine horizontal exchange between bdelloid individuals/species”** is not supported in that specific dataset [[12]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30017483/).

---

### G. Transposable element HGT in animals is widespread (especially insects)
Large-scale surveys find extensive TE movement across animal lineages.

- Across **195 insect genomes**, a PNAS study inferred **2,248 horizontal TE transfer events** over roughly the last 10 million years, with DNA transposons transferring more often than retrotransposons and with phylogenetic relatedness/geographic proximity influencing transfer likelihood [[100]](https://hal.science/hal-01513704v1/file/peccoud-et-al-2017-massive-horizontal-transfer-of-transposable-elements-in-insects.pdf).  
- Other landmark cases include repeated transfers of DNA transposons across tetrapods (e.g., SPIN) [[97]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/abs/10.1073/pnas.0809525105) and broad retrotransposon transfers (e.g., BovB, with ticks implicated as possible vectors) [[98]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1205856110).

As in plants, TE HGT may be a major genome-evolution force even when gene HGT is rare.

---

## 3) Which famous claims are now considered artifacts or at least heavily disputed?

### A. The tardigrade “one-sixth foreign genes” claim (Hypsibius dujardini): widely treated as contamination-driven
- A 2015 draft genome paper claimed ~17.5% of genes were likely HGT-derived [[10]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4702960/), and the journal record includes explicit criticisms framed as contamination and “no evidence for extensive HGT” [[9]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26598659/).  
- A later Genome Biology review on contamination detection describes this tardigrade HGT report as **artifactual due to overlooked bacterial contamination** [[41]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s13059-022-02619-9).

**Lesson:** eukaryotic assemblies (especially from non-axenic cultures) are contamination-prone; without stringent screening and read-level validation, HGT estimates can be grossly inflated [[41]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s13059-022-02619-9), [[49]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10898089/).

### B. Widespread HGT into the human genome: strong reanalyses argue “little or no evidence”
- A reanalysis argues that prominent claims of dozens to hundreds of human genes acquired by HGT are unsupported and better explained by gene loss/rate variation and other conventional processes [[13]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5422933/). (A separate entry reiterates the same critique framing against “HGT as a hallmark of the human genome”) [[43]](https://europepmc.org/article/pmc/5422933).

### C. Inflated interkingdom HGT counts in plants (many candidates do not survive reanalysis)
- A 2025 reassessment of published interkingdom plant HGT reports (1170 candidate genes) found only **29.3%** retained phylogenetic signal consistent with interkingdom HGT after updated taxon sampling/trees, and emphasized contamination/annotation errors and gene loss as major confounders [[2]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711439/).  
- The same reassessment states the **only well-documented natural interkingdom HGT** into plants is *Agrobacterium* T-DNA transfer [[2]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711439/)—a stricter position than some broader reviews.

**How to reconcile this with plant HGT reviews claiming “hundreds” of HGTs?**  
Because “HGT in plants” spans very different categories:
- **Plant-to-plant mitochondrial transfers** and **organelle capture** (often strong signals, different pitfalls) [[70]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15598737/), [[83]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24357311/), [[25]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3289295/).  
- **Parasitic plant nuclear HGT** (intragenic intron-retaining transfers; block transfers) [[90]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30407540/), [[91]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6294234/).  
- **Interkingdom nuclear HGT** (hardest to validate; highest false-positive risk), where stringent reassessment often reduces candidate lists [[2]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711439/).

---

## 4) How can HGT happen in plants/animals? (Mechanisms and enabling conditions)

### A. Vectors and interfaces that *physically* deliver DNA
1) **Agrobacterium (plants):** a natural DNA delivery and integration system [[28]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4127661/), [[27]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4426443/).  
2) **Viruses (animals and plants):** viruses can pick up host DNA and integrate into host genomes; a dedicated review argues viruses are likely major HT vectors in eukaryotes and frames transfer as “host→virus acquisition” plus “virus→host integration” [[34]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1879625716301900), [[69]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28672159/).  
   - Bracoviruses provide a clear example where integration into host DNA is part of the life cycle, and germline insertions can become heritable [[8]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4574769/).  
3) **Endosymbionts (animals):** intracellular bacteria like Wolbachia reside in close contact with host nuclei and can be present in germline tissues, increasing opportunities for nuclear integration [[31]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/), [[6]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2203625/).  
4) **Parasite–host cellular bridges:** haustoria in parasitic plants permit extensive molecular exchange, plausibly including DNA [[1]](https://www.mdpi.com/2075-1729/11/8/857), [[20]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8401529/).  
5) **Grafting and wound-induced cell fusion/porosity (plants):** graft unions generate cellular conditions (callus, pore formation) that permit organelle movement across species boundaries [[26]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7775762/), [[3]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1114076109).

### B. Why successful *heritable* HGT is rare (especially in animals)
- **Germline access barriers:** comparative analysis supports that more interdomain transfers occurred before evolution of a sequestered germline, consistent with germline sequestration blocking LGT in many bilaterian animals [[36]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27139503/).  
- **No dedicated HGT machinery:** unlike bacteria, eukaryotes typically lack specialized uptake/transfer systems, so transfers are often accidental and vector-mediated [[34]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1879625716301900).  
- **Regulatory/splicing compatibility:** foreign genes must work with eukaryotic promoters, chromatin, splicing, targeting, etc. Some transfers show rapid “domestication” signals (introns, polyadenylation, signal peptides), but most inserts likely die as pseudogenes [[6]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2203625/), [[58]](https://edepot.wur.nl/658009), [[59]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3306691/).  
- **Selection filters:** even if integration happens, fixation requires neutrality or benefit; “metabolic plug-ins” are more likely to be retained than genes requiring deep integration into developmental networks [[53]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf).

---

## 5) How common is HGT in plants and animals *really*? (What modern genomics suggests)

### Plants
**Not uniformly common**—highly clustered by genome compartment and ecology.
- **Mitochondria:** unusually HGT-prone in some lineages (Amborella is extreme) [[70]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15598737/), [[83]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24357311/).  
- **Parasitic plant nuclear genomes:** measurable but modest on average; genome surveys suggest ~**0.1–0.2%** of genes can be host-derived in some obligate parasites, with occasional large-block transfers [[90]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30407540/), [[20]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8401529/).  
- **Grasses:** some lineages show repeated nuclear HGT; in Alloteropsis, laterally acquired genes appear and disappear rapidly and contribute to pangenome variation [[40]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37793435/).  
- **TEs:** horizontal TE transfer is widespread across angiosperms in genome-scale surveys [[33]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4009612/).

### Animals
Most animal lineages show **low rates** of retained gene HGT, but there are **clear hotspots**:
- **Plant-parasitic nematodes:** repeated acquisitions of CAZymes and other genes tied to parasitism [[58]](https://edepot.wur.nl/658009), [[63]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC193618/), [[62]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3032686/), [[75]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3337135/).  
- **Certain insects:** diet/toxin arms races and symbiont/virus interactions can favor retention (whitefly detox gene; coffee borer mannanase) [[60]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867421001641), [[7]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22371593/).  
- **Bdelloid rotifers:** unusually high foreign gene content and functional expression, though exact percentages vary by method and dataset [[61]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23166508/), [[11]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4632278/).  
- **TE HGT:** very common in insects and detectable across broad animal clades [[100]](https://hal.science/hal-01513704v1/file/peccoud-et-al-2017-massive-horizontal-transfer-of-transposable-elements-in-insects.pdf), [[97]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/abs/10.1073/pnas.0809525105), [[98]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1205856110).

**Critical caution:** contamination is common in eukaryotic genome projects and can inflate HGT claims. NCBI’s large-scale screening work reports contaminants in roughly **one out of every three eukaryote genome submissions** in 2022 (per their summary table), motivating automated tools like FCS-GX [[49]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10898089/). A dedicated review emphasizes contamination can create famous false HGT narratives (tardigrades) [[41]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s13059-022-02619-9). Even outside eukaryotes, a 2026 analysis shows how contamination can generate pervasive false HGT networks if not rigorously checked [[77]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-026-69064-1).

---

## 6) Best-practice criteria for claiming HGT in plants/animals (and distinguishing it from look-alikes)

### A. Minimum evidence stack (increasing confidence)
1) **Candidate discovery**  
   - Similarity searches / “HGT index”-style screens can nominate candidates but are not sufficient alone [[10]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4702960/), [[48]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4155097/).  
2) **Phylogenetic validation**  
   - Show robust gene trees where the candidate nests within a distant donor clade with strong support (e.g., bootstrap thresholds are sometimes used operationally in organellar studies) [[46]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2022.988368/full).  
   - Explicitly consider alternatives: gene loss, rate variation, paralogy/duplication-loss, and poor taxon sampling [[1]](https://www.mdpi.com/2075-1729/11/8/857), [[2]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711439/), [[47]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4462595/).  
3) **Genome-context validation**
   - Demonstrate physical linkage to host genomic sequence (not just a contig with foreign genes), ideally with long reads, PCR, or consistent assembly support [[64]](https://journals.plos.org/plosgenetics/article), [[92]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3573108/).  
   - Use synteny/orthology carefully: synteny can help validate integration, but synteny analyses themselves depend on assembly/annotation quality and can mislead if HGT loci are included in phylogenomic inference [[50]](https://jlsteenwyk.com/publication_pdfs/2024_Steenwyk_and_King_PLOS_Biology.pdf).  
4) **Contamination exclusion**
   - Taxonomic profiling, coverage patterns, and database screening are essential because contamination is prevalent and can propagate through databases [[41]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s13059-022-02619-9), [[49]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10898089/).  
5) **Heritability / population coherence**
   - Show the locus segregates in expected phylogenetic patterns across individuals/populations (not just present in one sample), as in population-scale analyses of laterally acquired genes [[40]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37793435/).  
6) **Functional evidence (for adaptive significance)**
   - Expression (RNA-seq), proper splicing/polyadenylation, correct targeting signals [[58]](https://edepot.wur.nl/658009), [[59]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3306691/).  
   - Biochemical assays (enzyme activity) [[59]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3306691/), [[62]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3032686/).  
   - Genetic perturbation (RNAi/siRNA, knockout/knockdown) showing a phenotype [[60]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867421001641).  
   - Ecological linkage (diet, toxin defense, pathogens, parasitism) [[60]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867421001641), [[75]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3337135/), [[8]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4574769/).

### B. Distinguishing HGT from introgression/ILS (especially in plants)
In plants, **hybridization/introgression** is common and can mimic “foreign-like” phylogenetic placement. Site-pattern tools such as **D-statistics** and branch-length distribution methods like **QuIBL** are commonly used to separate introgression from incomplete lineage sorting (ILS) in discordant gene trees [[51]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12039212/).  
Additionally, “organelle capture” patterns (plastid phylogeny incongruent with nuclear phylogeny) can arise from hybridization—but graft-mediated plastid transfer provides a non-sexual alternative mechanism in some cases [[25]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3289295/).

---

## 7) When HGT is retained, what kinds of genes tend to “stick”? (Functional biases)

### Plants (retained HGT themes)
Broad analyses emphasize enrichment in:
- **Metabolism and transport**, including carbohydrate metabolism and metabolite movement [[55]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11131428/), [[54]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674205222000491).  
- **Stress responses** and environmental tolerance [[54]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674205222000491), [[68]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3907414/).  
- **Hormone-related functions** (e.g., auxin-biosynthesis enrichment among bacteria→plant transfers in one plant–microbiome study) [[55]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11131428/).  
- **Defense/conflict functions** in host–pathogen/host–consumer contexts [[53]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf).  
- And, very prominently, **transposable elements** (as cargo rather than phenotype-specific genes) [[20]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8401529/), [[33]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4009612/).

A particularly strong functional demonstration of cross-kingdom interchangeability is that a bacterial homologue of an essential Arabidopsis brassinosteroid gene (**DET2**) can complement a plant mutant and restore normal growth, illustrating how some foreign homologues can “plug in” to plant metabolic networks [[55]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11131428/).

### Animals (retained HGT themes)
Among the clearest adaptive cases, retained HGTs tend to encode:
- **Digestive and metabolic enzymes** enabling new diets (cell wall polysaccharides, mannan) [[58]](https://edepot.wur.nl/658009), [[59]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3306691/), [[75]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3337135/).  
- **Detoxification** of plant defenses (whitefly malonyltransferase) [[60]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867421001641).  
- **Pigment biosynthesis** (carotenoids from fungi) [[56]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20431015/), [[57]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3297373/).  
- **Immune/conflict-related functions**, sometimes via viral intermediates (bracovirus domestication; pathogen protection) [[8]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4574769/).  
- In some lineages (bdelloids), large suites of foreign **enzymes** contribute broadly to metabolism and stress/pathogen responses [[61]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23166508/), [[76]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-49919-1).

This “metabolic plug-in” bias is consistent with the idea that genes whose products function relatively modularly (enzymes, transporters, secreted effectors) are more likely to become useful after transfer than genes embedded in complex developmental regulatory networks [[53]](https://www3.botany.ubc.ca/keeling/PDF/24HGT.pdf).

---

## 8) So what’s the “interesting reason” HGT persists despite being rare?
The most consistent evolutionary logic across plants and animals is:

1) **Opportunities are rare and highly structured**  
   HGT is concentrated where biology creates unusual DNA delivery routes: **parasite bridges**, **viral integration**, **endosymbionts in germline-adjacent tissues**, **wounds/grafts**, **organelle fusion** [[20]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8401529/), [[8]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4574769/), [[31]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3068243/), [[26]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7775762/), [[83]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24357311/).

2) **Selection can be extremely strong once a useful function appears**  
   A single successful transfer can unlock:
   - a new **food source** (coffee beans via bacterial mannanase) [[59]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3306691/),  
   - a new **host range** or toxin tolerance (whitefly detox gene) [[60]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867421001641),  
   - a new **parasitic capability** (nematode cellulases/pectinases) [[58]](https://edepot.wur.nl/658009), [[75]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3337135/),  
   - improved performance of a complex trait (C4 enzyme optimization in grasses) [[74]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7751175/),  
   - or new **defense chemistry** (bdelloid antibiotic-like pathways responding to pathogens) [[76]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-49919-1).

3) **Many HGT events probably happen but most don’t “graduate” to long-term inheritance**
   Endosymbiont DNA inserts (e.g., Wolbachia fragments) show DNA integration can occur but remain largely pseudogenized [[6]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2203625/). The rarity is not only transfer—it’s **successful integration + heritability + retention**.

---

## 9) Major debates and frontier directions as of 2026

### A. How much eukaryotic nuclear HGT is “real” vs artifact?
- Reviews acknowledge a long-running debate, with some arguing that widespread nuclear HGT is over-inferred, and others arguing it is real but unevenly distributed across eukaryotes [[67]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41576-023-00688-5).  
- Methodologically, plant interkingdom HGT catalogs shrink substantially under reassessment, suggesting a high false-positive rate for some claim types [[2]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711439/).  
- The contamination literature treats false HGT narratives as a recurring failure mode and shows contamination can dominate inference if not aggressively managed [[41]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s13059-022-02619-9), [[49]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10898089/), [[77]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-026-69064-1).

### B. What are the dominant vectors—viruses, TEs, symbionts, or cell–cell bridges?
- Viruses are increasingly argued to be major HT vectors in eukaryotes, especially for TEs [[69]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28672159/), [[34]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1879625716301900).  
- Bracoviruses provide a rare, well-characterized animal system where the vector is explicit and heritable insertion is demonstrable [[8]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4574769/).  
- For plants, the frontier includes quantifying how often grafting/natural root grafts enable organelle movement in nature (the mechanism is now plausible and experimentally observed) [[26]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7775762/), [[25]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3289295/).

### C. From detection to causality: proving fitness effects in natural contexts
The most compelling future work will combine:
- long-read assemblies + population data (to prove integration and inheritance),  
- expression and biochemical assays (to prove function), and  
- ecological experiments (to prove fitness advantage).  
Whitefly BtPMaT1 is a model for this gold-standard pipeline [[60]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867421001641); many parasitic plant nuclear HGT cases are now ready for similar functional tests [[90]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30407540/), [[89]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-018-04344-z), [[72]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26051213/).

### D. Implications for biotechnology and biosafety
- Natural Agrobacterium-derived transgenes in crops (sweet potato) show that cross-kingdom gene movement is not purely artificial [[27]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4426443/).  
- Understanding natural HGT routes (bacteria, viruses, graft unions) informs risk assessment, especially for gene movement potential across ecological networks [[71]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12451028/), [[69]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28672159/).

---

## Bottom line
- **HGT in plants and animals is rare in the sense that most genes still evolve vertically**, but it is **not negligible**: it is concentrated in specific **interfaces (parasitism, symbiosis, viruses, graft unions)** and in specific **genomic cargo (organellar DNA, TEs, modular metabolic enzymes)** [[20]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8401529/), [[8]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4574769/), [[33]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4009612/), [[25]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3289295/).  
- When retained, HGT can be evolutionarily outsized—enabling **new diets, new host ranges, new parasite capabilities, improved photosynthetic biochemistry, and new defense strategies** [[60]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867421001641), [[59]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3306691/), [[75]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3337135/), [[74]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7751175/), [[76]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-49919-1).  
- The field’s biggest challenge is not finding candidates—it is **rigorous validation** against contamination and alternative evolutionary histories, and moving from “plausible transfer” to **demonstrated function and fitness advantage** using modern genomic and experimental toolchains [[41]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s13059-022-02619-9), [[49]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10898089/), [[2]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711439/), [[60]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867421001641).
